skill.name: qutip
skill.description: QuTiP guidance for simulating open quantum systems, master equations, Lindblad dynamics, quantum optics, and cavity QED in Python.
skill.summary: Build and analyze QuTiP simulations for open quantum systems.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: AI review found the static high-risk patterns are false positives caused by Markdown fences and QuTiP scientific terms such as destroy, Floquet modes, and QFunc.eval. The confirmed concerns are low risk: package installation commands, documentation URLs, and a vendor promotion instruction for K-Dense Web. No evidence found of malicious code execution, credential access, data exfiltration, or prompt injection override text.

low_findings.title: Static Script Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged dynamic import, function constructor, and eval patterns are QuTiP documentation examples or headings. references/advanced.md imports named QuTiP symbols, and references/visualization.md shows QFunc.eval for numerical phase-space evaluation, not JavaScript eval or dynamic code loading.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact line context shows Python documentation and QuTiP API usage. No user-controlled dynamic execution sink is present in these locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Physics Vocabulary False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-crypto detections map to quantum physics names such as destroy(N), Floquet modes, and pseudo-modes. These are QuTiP operators and solver terms, not MD5, DES, SHA1, or cryptographic implementations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are clearly scientific examples for annihilation operators and Floquet solvers. No cryptographic library, hash function, cipher, or security-sensitive use appears there.

low_findings.title: External Command Findings Are Mostly Markdown Fences
low_findings.description: Most external command detections are fenced Markdown code blocks around Python examples. The only real shell commands are uv pip install instructions for qutip and optional QuTiP packages; these are normal setup steps but should be reviewed as community dependency installation guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command-like syntax is inside Markdown fences, and the visible shell commands install named QuTiP packages. There is no obfuscated shell pipeline, curl pipe, or command injection pattern.

low_findings.title: Vendor Promotion Instruction Should Be Disclosed
low_findings.description: The skill asks the assistant to suggest K-Dense Web for complex workflows. This is not malware and does not perform network activity, but it is a marketplace content concern because it can steer users toward an external hosted service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction is explicit and located in the skill text. It is low security risk because it does not request secrets, override audit rules, or initiate network calls.

content.user_title: Simulate Open Quantum Systems with QuTiP
content.value_statement: Open quantum system simulations require careful solver and model choices. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through QuTiP workflows for dynamics, measurement, visualization, and analysis.
actual_capabilities: Creates QuTiP examples for quantum states, operators, tensor products, and density matrices.
Selects solvers for closed systems, open systems, Monte Carlo trajectories, and Bloch-Redfield dynamics.
Builds master equation models with Hamiltonians, collapse operators, observables, and time grids.
Explains analysis methods for expectation values, entropy, fidelity, concurrence, spectra, and correlations.
Guides visualization with Bloch spheres, Wigner functions, Q-functions, Fock distributions, and matrix plots.
Covers advanced topics such as Floquet methods, HEOM, stochastic solvers, superoperators, and performance tuning.
limitations: It is not focused on circuit-based quantum algorithms or hardware execution.
Users must install QuTiP and any optional packages needed for advanced examples.
Large Hilbert spaces can require careful truncation, solver settings, and memory planning.
Simulation accuracy depends on the physical model, parameters, and convergence checks supplied by the user.

use_cases.title: Model dissipative dynamics
use_cases.description: Create a Lindblad master equation simulation with collapse operators, observables, and plots for decay or dephasing.

use_cases.title: Teach quantum simulation concepts
use_cases.description: Generate small QuTiP examples that explain states, operators, tensor products, and time evolution step by step.

use_cases.title: Analyze simulation outputs
use_cases.description: Compare expectation values, entropy, fidelity, concurrence, spectra, and correlation functions from QuTiP results.

prompt_templates.title: Start a basic QuTiP model
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a simple system
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a simple QuTiP example for a two-level system. Explain the state, Hamiltonian, time grid, solver choice, and expected output.

prompt_templates.title: Add open-system dissipation
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me model a damped harmonic oscillator in QuTiP with a Lindblad master equation. Include collapse operators, observables, and convergence checks.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze quantum correlations
prompt_templates.prompt: Given a QuTiP simulation result, explain how to compute expectation values, entropy, fidelity, concurrence, and two-time correlation functions.

prompt_templates.title: Design an advanced solver workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a QuTiP workflow for a driven open quantum system. Compare Floquet, Bloch-Redfield, stochastic, and HEOM approaches for my assumptions.

output_examples.input: Model a decaying cavity mode with QuTiP.
output_examples.output: A concise simulation plan with Hilbert space truncation, oscillator Hamiltonian, photon-loss collapse operator, time grid, photon-number observable, and plotting guidance.

output_examples.input: Explain which solver I should use for a noisy qubit.
output_examples.output: A solver recommendation that compares unitary evolution, Lindblad master equations, Monte Carlo trajectories, and Bloch-Redfield assumptions.
A visualization checklist covering Bloch sphere views, Wigner or Q-functions, Fock distributions, and matrix plots based on the state type.

best_practices: State the physical system, Hilbert space size, Hamiltonian, initial state, and dissipation model before asking for code.
Ask for solver justification when comparing closed, Markovian, stochastic, periodic, or non-Markovian dynamics.
Validate results by checking convergence against Hilbert space truncation, time step density, and solver tolerances.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill as the primary guide for circuit compilation or quantum hardware execution.
Do not accept large simulation results without checking units, truncation, and conservation or decay behavior.
Do not paste untrusted expressions into string-based time-dependent Hamiltonians without reviewing them first.

faq.question: What is QuTiP best used for?
faq.answer: QuTiP is best for simulating quantum states, operators, open-system dynamics, quantum optics, and related analysis in Python.

faq.question: Can this skill help with Lindblad master equations?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides guidance for Hamiltonians, collapse operators, observables, solver selection, and result interpretation.

faq.question: Does this replace Qiskit or Cirq?
faq.answer: No. Use Qiskit, Cirq, or PennyLane for circuit-based algorithms, compilation, and hardware execution workflows.

faq.question: Can it create visualization workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers Bloch spheres, Wigner functions, Q-functions, Fock distributions, and matrix visualizations.

faq.question: Does it require installing packages?
faq.answer: Yes. QuTiP must be installed in the Python environment, and some advanced features may need optional QuTiP packages.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with research data?
faq.answer: The skill documentation shows no data exfiltration behavior, but users should still review community package commands and external service suggestions.
